title: The Tennis Ball on the Floor: A Pelvic Practice
dek: *You've been taught that anger lives in your head, that it's a thought problem requiring cognitive solutions. But your pelvis knows differently. It holds the story your mind tries to forget, and a single tennis ball on the floor can help you tell it.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Placing a tennis ball under your sacrum while lying down creates targeted pressure that releases pelvic tension where anger often lodges. This simple floor practice combines somatic awareness with fascial release to help discharge stored emotional energy. The technique takes five minutes and requires nothing but a standard tennis ball and a willingness to feel.

No one warned you that the body keeps the score in your hips. ## Why the Pelvic Floor Becomes an Anger Repository Your pelvic floor is a hammock of muscles, fascia, and connective tissue that spans the base of your pelvis. It supports your bladder, bowel, and reproductive organs. It also happens to be one of the primary storage sites for unprocessed emotional material, particularly anger that you've been socially conditioned to swallow. When you clench your jaw during a meeting where someone takes credit for your work, your pelvic floor often mirrors that tension. When you smile through a family dinner while rage simmers underneath, those muscles contract. Over time, this chronic holding pattern becomes your body's default, a kind of muscular amnesia that forgets how to release. Peter Levine's work on trauma and the body emphasizes that incomplete defensive responses, the fight-or-flight reactions we abort midstream, leave residue in our tissues. Your pelvis, positioned at the body's gravitational center, becomes a collection point for these unfinished stories. The anger you didn't express at twelve is still there, coded in muscle fiber and fascial adhesion. In my own practice, I've watched people discover sensation in their pelvic floor for the first time in decades. The shock of recognition, oh, I've been holding here, is often followed by tears, laughter, or a sudden need to move. Your body has been waiting for permission to release what your mind deemed unacceptable. ## The Tennis Ball Technique: What It Actually Does This isn't massage. It's not stretching. It's a form of myofascial release that uses sustained pressure to encourage your nervous system to let go. The tennis ball, positioned under your sacrum (the triangular bone at the base of your spine), creates a focal point that your body can't ignore. The pressure triggers mechanoreceptors in your fascia, specialized nerve endings that communicate with your brain about tissue state. When these receptors register sustained, non-threatening pressure, they signal safety. Your nervous system, reassured that you're not in danger, can begin to release the protective holding pattern it's maintained. You're essentially having a conversation with your pelvis in a language older than words. The ball says, "I'm here, I'm steady, you can soften." Your tissues, given time and attention, respond. The release isn't always dramatic. Sometimes it's a subtle warmth, a feeling of expansion, a quiet sigh that moves through your lower body like a wave. What makes this practice particularly effective for anger work is its specificity. Unlike general relaxation techniques, the tennis ball targets the exact geography where anger tends to crystallize. You're not trying to think your way out of tension. You're meeting it directly, with pressure and presence. ## Setting Up Your Practice Space You need a firm surface. Carpet over concrete works well. Hardwood is fine. Your bed is too soft and will absorb the pressure that should be going into your tissue. Lay down a yoga mat if you have one, but it's not essential. Place the tennis ball on the floor. Sit down in front of it, then slowly lower yourself so the ball makes contact with your sacrum. This might take some adjustment. You're looking for the flat, bony triangle at the base of your spine, just above your tailbone. The ball should rest in the natural hollow there, not on your spine itself. Your legs can be straight or bent, whichever feels more sustainable. Some people prefer knees bent with feet flat on the floor. Others extend their legs fully. Listen to what your body wants today, not what it wanted last week. The room should be warm enough that you're not bracing against cold. Dim lighting helps. You might want a pillow under your head. This isn't about suffering through discomfort. It's about creating conditions where your nervous system feels safe enough to release. Before you begin, set a timer for five minutes. This gives you permission to stay without watching the clock. Your only job is to breathe and notice. ## The Five-Minute Practice Lie back with the tennis ball under your sacrum. Take three full breaths, inhaling through your nose and exhaling through your mouth. Let the weight of your body settle onto the ball. Notice the pressure. Is it sharp? Dull? Does it radiate? Where does the sensation travel? Your job isn't to change anything, just to observe with curiosity. Imagine you're a scientist documenting the landscape of your own pelvis. After about a minute, you might notice the pressure beginning to soften. This is your tissue starting to release. Stay with it. If emotions arise, sobbing, anger, grief, let them move through. Your tennis ball doesn't judge. Around the three-minute mark, many people experience a shift. The sensation might intensify briefly before dispersing. You might feel warmth spreading through your hips. You might suddenly remember something you haven't thought about in years. This is normal. Your body is speaking. In the final minute, focus on your breath again. Notice if it's deeper than when you started. Notice if your hips feel different, heavier, more settled, somehow more yours. When the timer sounds, roll slowly to one side. Take your time sitting up. Stand slowly. Walk around your space and notice how your pelvis feels in motion. Different? More fluid? That's your anger beginning to move again. ## What You Might Feel (And What It Means) The first time you do this, you might feel nothing. That's information too. Numbness is a protective strategy, and it takes time to thaw. Keep practicing. Sensation will emerge. You might feel intense discomfort that makes you want to bail immediately. That's your nervous system testing whether it's safe to stay. If you can breathe through the first thirty seconds, the intensity usually softens. If it doesn't, remove the ball. This practice should never feel like punishment. Some people experience emotional release, tears, anger, laughter, seemingly unrelated to anything. Karla McLaren's research into emotional flow reminds us that emotions are messengers carrying specific information, and anger in particular signals that a boundary has been violated. When your pelvis begins to release stored anger, the emotion that emerges isn't always proportional to the present moment. You might find yourself furious about something that happened decades ago. Let it come. Others feel physical sensations that travel. The pressure under your sacrum might send sensation down your legs, up your spine, into your belly. This is your fascia communicating, passing messages through the connective tissue web that links your entire body. Follow the sensation with your attention, like watching a river flow. Over the years I've seen people stand up from this practice and immediately need to stomp their feet, punch a pillow, or go for a run. The anger that was locked in static holding has become kinetic energy again. Movement is completion. Let yourself move. ## Integrating This Practice Into Your Anger Work The tennis ball practice isn't meant to stand alone. It's one tool in a larger ecology of anger practices, which you'll find explored in depth across the articles we've published on this site, particularly in [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) we've assembled for exactly this kind of somatic work. Think of this as physical preparation for emotional expression. When your pelvis is chronically tight, your anger has nowhere to move. It circles back on itself, becoming depression, anxiety, or physical pain. By creating space in your tissues, you're literally making room for anger to flow through and out. Pair this practice with voice work. After releasing tension in your pelvis, try growling, roaring, or speaking the things you've never said aloud. The combination of physical release and vocal expression creates a complete circuit. Your anger can finally move from storage to expression to completion. You might also explore how different herbs support this kind of release work. In [our herbal cabinet](/herbs), we discuss plants that help help emotional movement without suppressing the feeling itself. [Plant Therapy Organic Bergamot (10 mL)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01M5D2B3X?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be particularly helpful here, its citrus brightness creating an olfactory anchor that signals safety to your nervous system while you do deeper tissue work. ## The Fascial Web and Emotional Storage Your fascia is a continuous network of connective tissue that wraps every muscle, organ, and bone in your body. It's not inert packing material. It's a sensory organ in its own right, densely populated with nerve endings and capable of contracting independently of muscle. When you experience chronic stress or suppress emotion repeatedly, your fascia responds by thickening and adhering. Areas that should glide smoothly become stuck. The fluid matrix that normally allows easy movement becomes viscous, more gel than liquid. Your pelvis, bearing the weight of your upper body and mediating the force of your legs, is particularly vulnerable to this fascial restriction. What's remarkable is that fascia appears to store emotional memory. Release a tight spot in your hip and a memory surfaces. Press into your sacrum and suddenly you're feeling the anger from a conversation ten years ago. This isn't mystical. It's neurobiology. Your nervous system encodes experience throughout your body, not just in your brain. The tennis ball practice works directly with this fascial web. The sustained pressure creates a piezoelectric effect, a subtle electrical charge that encourages fascial tissue to reorganize and hydrate. As the tissue softens, the emotional charge it's been holding can discharge too. Soraya Chemaly's cultural analysis of women's anger points out that girls are socialized to contract rather than expand when angry, to make themselves smaller rather than larger. This physical compression, repeated over years, becomes fascial restriction. Your body literally shapes itself around the anger you weren't allowed to express. The tennis ball helps you reclaim that space. ## Contraindications and When to Modify This practice isn't appropriate for everyone. If you're pregnant, skip it. The pressure on your sacrum affects your entire pelvic bowl, and you don't want to create instability in a structure that's already adapting to carry new life. If you have a herniated disc, severe sciatica, or active pelvic floor dysfunction, consult a pelvic floor physical therapist before trying this. The tennis ball is generally safe, but your situation might require a different approach or professional guidance first. People with a trauma history involving sexual violence should approach this practice with particular care. Directing attention to your pelvis can be activating. Consider working with a trauma-informed somatic therapist who can help you titrate the experience, building your capacity gradually rather than overwhelming your system. If you have osteoporosis or take blood thinners, use a softer ball. A partially deflated tennis ball or a small therapy ball designed for myofascial release will give you the benefits without the risk of bruising or bone stress. Listen to the difference between discomfort and pain. Discomfort is your body adjusting to new sensation. Pain is your body saying stop. Honor that boundary. You can always return to this practice tomorrow with less pressure or shorter duration. ## The Anger-Pelvic Floor Connection in Research While the specific tennis ball technique isn't widely studied, the broader relationship between emotional suppression and pelvic floor tension is well documented. The APA's research summary on anger management acknowledges that unexpressed anger contributes to physical symptoms including muscle tension, particularly in the jaw, shoulders, and pelvic region. Pelvic floor physical therapists routinely encounter patients whose muscle tension has no structural cause. The tissues are healthy but chronically contracted. When these patients begin addressing emotional material, particularly anger and grief, their pelvic floor symptoms often improve without direct physical intervention. The field of somatic psychology has long recognized that the pelvis holds what the conscious mind disowns. Wilhelm Reich, controversial as his later work became, correctly identified the pelvic segment as a primary site of what he called "muscular armor," the body's physical manifestation of psychological defense. Contemporary research into adverse childhood experiences shows that early trauma correlates with adult pelvic pain conditions, endometriosis, vulvodynia, chronic pelvic pain syndrome. The mechanism isn't fully understood, but the pattern is clear: unprocessed emotional experience, especially anger that couldn't be safely expressed, manifests in the pelvis. This doesn't mean your physical symptoms are "all in your head." It means your body and mind are inseparable, and healing requires addressing both. The tennis ball is a bridge between psychological insight and physical release. ## Beyond the Ball: Expanding Your Practice Once you're comfortable with the basic technique, you can explore variations. Try placing the ball slightly to one side of your sacrum, then the other. Notice if one side feels tighter, more tender, more resistant to release. That asymmetry is information about how you've been holding yourself. You can also experiment with movement while on the ball. Small pelvic tilts, gentle rocking side to side, micro-movements that explore the pressure rather than escaping it. This active approach can help faster release for some people. Pair the physical practice with guided imagery. While lying on the ball, imagine your anger as a color, a shape, a texture. Watch it move through your body. Give it permission to be exactly what it is without needing to change it. Sometimes naming and witnessing is enough to complete the emotional arc. Consider exploring complementary practices that address anger from other angles. [Spiritual Bypassing - Robert Augustus Masters](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1622038371?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers crucial insight into how we use spiritual practices to avoid rather than process difficult emotions. If you find yourself using the tennis ball practice to "transcend" your anger rather than feel it, this book will help you course-correct. For moments when you need additional support, [Bach Original Flower Remedies, Holly (Non-Alcohol Formula) - Goodwill Towards Ot](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B09HH1D3WS?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help soften the edges of intense anger, not by suppressing it but by helping you stay present with the feeling long enough to let it move through. ## Three Questions People Actually Ask ### How often should I do this practice? Daily is ideal when you're first establishing the practice, even if only for five minutes. Your nervous system learns through repetition, and consistent practice teaches your pelvis that it's safe to release. After a few weeks, you might shift to three or four times a week, or use it as needed when you notice tension accumulating. Some people keep a tennis ball by their bed and do a quick session before sleep. Others prefer morning practice to clear residual tension from the night. Trust your body's feedback about frequency. ### Can I use something other than a tennis ball? Yes, with considerations. A lacrosse ball is firmer and creates more intense pressure, appropriate if you have significant tissue density or if the tennis ball feels too soft. A partially deflated playground ball or a specialized therapy ball like a Yoga Tune Up ball offers a gentler option. Avoid anything with an irregular surface or anything so soft it collapses completely under your weight. The ball needs to maintain enough structure to create sustained pressure. Whatever you use, make sure it's clean and won't roll away unexpectedly. ### What if I feel worse after doing this? Sometimes releasing tension brings temporary discomfort as your body adjusts to new patterns. You might feel sore, emotionally raw, or more aware of tension in other areas. This is often a sign that the practice is working, that you're thawing frozen tissue and emotion. However, if the discomfort is severe or persists beyond a day or two, you might be pressing too hard or holding too long. Scale back the pressure or duration. If symptoms continue, consult a pelvic floor physical therapist. Healing isn't linear, and sometimes what looks like regression is actually your body asking for a different approach. ## The Practice That Keeps Practicing You Here's what nobody tells you about somatic work: once you begin, your body won't let you stop halfway. The tennis ball becomes a portal, and walking through it means committing to the conversation your pelvis wants to have. This isn't a one-time fix. It's a practice that evolves as you evolve, revealing new layers as you're ready to meet them. You might find that after months of regular practice, the ball stops being about anger entirely. It becomes a check-in, a way of listening to what your body knows before your mind catches up. Or you might discover that your pelvis was only the beginning, that releasing tension there opens access to your diaphragm, your throat, your jaw. The body tells its story in sequence, one chapter opening the next. For more practices that honor this sequential unfolding, explore [the assessments hub](/assessments) where we've gathered tools for tracking your own patterns of holding and release. You might also find resonance with the broader anger practices we discuss at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), where the focus shifts from technique to the deeper philosophical questions about what anger is for and how it shapes a life. The floor is waiting. The ball is simple. Your pelvis has been holding this conversation in reserve for years. All you have to do is lie down and listen. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on the tennis ball on the floor: a pelvic practice, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
